author: sky.cactus
content:
There is talk about moving away from multi-client and towards formal proofs.  I don't think this change is bad on its own, but I want to make sure it is being framed correctly.  The reason that 3 independently developed clients is good is because you have 3 independent agents (humans historically) interpreting the spec and translating that to a more formal language (code).  You then run these 3 clients against each other and make sure they all produce the same output.

Those 3 independent agents generating 3 codifications of a specification is the important part here, not that this step occurs in client code or somewhere else.  You can achieve similar results by having one independent agent writing tests and the other writing code and then anytime there is disagreement the two agents discuss and figure out who is correct.  With formal proofs, you have a 3rd place where you can have some agent turn spec into something more formal, so you can achieve the same security guarantees as 3 independent clients by having 1 formal proof writing agent, 1 test writing agent, 1 client writing agent.

What I worry about though, and what I would like to make sure we avoid, is that we move to a model where one agent (human or AI) turns a spec into a formal proof and then we *blindly* implement clients and tests against that formal proof.  For this "3 independent agents" thing to work, it is absolutely critical that none of the 3 are authoritative.  When there is disagreement between the 3 things it **MUST** be resolved via collaboration among authors to determine which is correct.

author: sky.cactus
content: I can totally see a future where all 3 of these implementers are AI agents, but as with humans, they must have independent/different contexts!  You do not gain the robustness of 3 independent implementations by having one person implement something 3 times, because they are very likely to make the same mistake 3 times.
